doc_findings:
The examination is affected by artifacts. Visualization is decreased:

Lungs and pleura: Centrilobular emphysema in the lungs (more pronounced in the upper lobes). Mild areas of pleuroapical fibrosis.

Right lung:
Previously noted solid nodule in the apical regions with increased size to 2.3 × 1.7 cm, previously up to 1.8 × 1.6 cm by independent repeat measurement. The margins of the above-described nodule are spiculated, including a thickened band extending to the costal pleura.
In the lower lobe, two adjacent solid nodules measure up to 2 mm (series 5 image 181) and were not clearly differentiated previously.
Calcified granuloma in the lower lobe measuring up to 4 mm.

Left lung:
In the left upper lobe, a calcified lesion with thin peripheral linear bands is present (no change), series 5 image 111.

Right pleural effusion with layer thickness up to 1.8 cm (previously absent). No left pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and lung hila: No mass lesions or lymphadenopathy. Scattered intrathoracic lymph nodes with calcifications.

Heart: Cardiac chambers are mildly enlarged. Distal portions of pacemaker leads (generator most recently implanted in the soft tissues of the left pectoral region) are in the right cardiac chambers. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Moderate coronary calcification. Aortic valve calcification.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is dilated (4.2 cm) with wall calcification. The pulmonary trunk is dilated (3.3 cm).

Osseous structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation. Decreased vertebral body density at the T11-L2 levels up to +126 HU.

Included abdomen: Visualization is decreased due to motion artifacts. Areas of pancreatic lipomatosis.
doc_impression:
1.

Right upper lobe nodule has increased in size. Findings are suspicious for a neoplastic etiology. Morphologic verification is recommended. Consider positron emission tomography-computed tomography. New solid nodules in the right lower lobe are present; given the above, a secondary etiology cannot be excluded. Single calcified lesion in the left upper lobe shows no change and may correspond to a healed granuloma; no follow-up is required. Pulmonary emphysema. Small right pleural effusion (previously not present).

2.

Pulmonary trunk dilation up to 3.4 cm. This may be a sign of pulmonary hypertension.

3.

Moderate coronary calcification. Pacemaker placement.

4.

Aortic valve calcification.

5.

Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta with aneurysmal dilation of the ascending thoracic aorta.

6.

Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation. CT findings of osteopenia at the T11-L2 levels.

TECHNIQUE: Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume {dose_ctdi} mGy; total DLP {dose_dlp} mGy-cm

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months:
val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Severe centrilobular emphysema in the lungs (more pronounced in the upper lobes), moderate paraseptal emphysema. Pleuroapical scarring. Calcified granuloma in both lungs measure up to 4 mm, unchanged.
Previously noted solid spiculated nodule in the apical regions of the right lung with increased size to 2.3 × 1.7 cm, previously up to 1.8 × 1.6 cm. Along the periphery of the nodule, areas of ground glass increased in number and size.
In the right lower lobe, two adjacent solid nodules measure up to 2 mm (series 5 image 181), previously not determined.
In the left upper lobe, a calcified lesion with thin peripheral linear bands is present (no change), series 5 image 117.
In the left upper lobe, a solid nodule with thin peripheral linear bands is present (no change), series 5 image 112.
Right pleural effusion with layer thickness up to 1.8 cm (previously absent). No left pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and lung hila: No mass lesions or lymphadenopathy. Mediastinal lymph nodes with calcifications, consequences of a previous granulomatous disease.

Heart: Left ventricle is mildly enlarged. Distal portions of pacemaker leads are in the right cardiac chambers. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Moderate coronary calcification. Aortic valve calcification.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is dilated (4.2 cm) with moderate wall calcification. The pulmonary trunk is dilated (3.4 cm).

Osseous structures and chest wall: Severe degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation. Decreased vertebral body density at the T11-L2 levels up to 126 HU (osteopenia). Pacemaker implanted in the soft tissues of the left pectoral region.

Included abdomen: Areas of pancreatic lipomatosis.
val_impression:
1. Increased in size, right upper lobe nodule, suspicious for a neoplastic etiology. New two solid nodules in the right lower lobe. Other solid and calcified nodules are stable.  

2. Small right pleural effusion (previously not present).

3. Severe mixed emphysema.

4. Pulmonary trunk dilation up to 3.4 cm may be a sign of pulmonary hypertension.

5. Moderate coronary calcification. 

6. Aortic valve calcification. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta with dilation of the ascending aorta. Enlargement of the left ventricle.

INDICATION: Solitary pulmonary nodule.

COMPARISON: Chest CT on November 19, 2025.

TECHNIQUE: Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume 14.45 mGy; total DLP 562.66 mGy-cm

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 1.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura:  
A previously identified nodule in the left lower lobe is no longer seen. A 4 mm solid subpleural nodule in the right middle lobe (series 5, image 141), with a subtle increase in size. Linear atelectasis in the lingular segments (clinically not significant). There are solitary perifissural solid nodules up to 2 mm, polygonal in shape, likely intrapulmonary lymph nodes. Persistent areas of reticular opacity in the supradiaphragmatic portions of both lungs, likely atelectatic changes (clinically not significant). No pleural effusion. Pleuroapical pleural thickening bilaterally.

Mediastinum and lung hila: No mass. No lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Moderate coronary calcification.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated, with calcification of the wall of the descending segment. The pulmonary trunk is dilated (3.2 cm).

Osseous structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Anterolisthesis of the T2 and T4 vertebrae by 3 mm. Decreased density of vertebral bodies: T11 - 135 HU, T12 - 118 HU.

Included abdomen: Unremarkable.
doc_impression:
1. A 4 mm solid nodule in the right middle lobe with interval growth. Per Fleischner Society 2017 recommendations, consider chest CT in 12 months.

2. Complete regression of the previously identified nodule in the left lower lobe.

3. Dilated pulmonary trunk. This may be consistent with pulmonary arterial hypertension.

4. Moderate coronary calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

5. Thoracic aortic calcification.

6. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Anterolisthesis of the T2 and T4 vertebrae. Findings of osteopenia.

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume {dose_ctdi} mGy; total DLP {dose_dlp} mGy-cm

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months:
val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: A previously identified nodule in the left lower lobe is no longer seen. A 4 mm part solid subpleural nodule in the right middle lobe (series 5, image 141), with an increase in size of less than 1 mm. There are solitary perifissural solid nodules up to 2 mm, polygonal in shape, likely intrapulmonary lymph nodes, require no follow up. A 3 mm calcified granuloma in the middle lobe. require no follow up. Thickening of the interlobular interstitium at the lung bases, unchanged. In the lower lobes, peribronchial areas have areas of ground glass, unchanged. Linear atelectasis and small bronchiectasis in the lingular segments (clinically insignificant). No pleural effusion. Pleuroapical scarring bilaterally.

Mediastinum and lung hila: No mass. No lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Moderate coronary calcification.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated, with mild calcification of the wall of the descending segment. The pulmonary trunk is dilated (3.9 cm).

Osseous structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Degenerative changes of the shoulder joints. Decreased density of vertebral bodies: T11 - 135 HU, T12 - 118 HU. Hemangioma in the T12 vertebral body requires no follow up.

Included abdomen: Unremarkable.
val_impression:
1. Complete regression of the previously identified nodule in the left lower lobe. A 4 mm part solid nodule in the right middle lobe with small interval growth. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following: No need for routine follow-up. However, if the nodule has suspicious morphology or if the patient belongs to higher risk population group, consider follow-up chest CT at 2 and 4 years. 

2. Thickening of the interlobular interstitium and areas of ground glass at the lung bases, unchanged, may be associated with heart disease or interstitial changes.

3. Dilated pulmonary trunk (3.9 cm). This may be consistent with pulmonary arterial hypertension.

4. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

5. Thoracic aortic calcification.

6. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Degenerative changes of the shoulder joints. Decreased vertebral body density consistent with osteoporosis. Hemangioma in the T12 vertebral body requires no follow up.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura: No nodules requiring follow-up are identified. Mild linear atelectasis in the right middle lobe and in the left basal segments. Otherwise, the lungs are unremarkable.  
No pleural effusion.

Trachea and central bronchi: Patent. No endoluminal bronchial contents are identified. In the upper third of the trachea (subglottic level/at the level of the thyroid gland, series 4, image 19), a right anterolateral wall diverticulum is identified measuring up to 4 mm.

Mediastinum and hila: Residual thymic tissue. Otherwise, the mediastinum is unremarkable. No mass and no lymphadenopathy are identified.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. No coronary calcification is identified.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Osseous structures and chest wall: Mild degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with sclerosis of the subchondral portions of the vertebral bodies.

Included upper abdomen: Unremarkable.
doc_impression:
1. No nodules requiring follow-up per Fleischner Society recommendations are identified. Small diverticulum of the upper third of the trachea measuring up to 4 mm.

2. Mild degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.

doc_findings:
Calcified granuloma in the right lower lobe, measures up to 2 mm (series 5, image 78). Plate-like atelectasis in the right upper lobe (series 5, images 103-115). Plate-like atelectasis in the middle upper lobe (series 5, images 140-158).

Mediastinum and lung hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy. Calcification in a nonenlarged subcarinal lymph node.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Coronary calcification, qualitatively assessed as mild. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated. Pulmonary trunk is not dilated. Aortic walls with a few calcified plaques.

Osseous structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Single 2 mm calcification in the right breast.

Included abdomen: Cholecystectomy.
doc_impression:
1. Calcified granuloma in the right lower lobe and calcification of mediastinal lymph nodes, consistent with prior infection.

2. Plate-like atelectasis in the right upper lobe and middle upper lobe.

3. Mild coronary calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

4. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

5. Single calcification in the right breast.

6. Cholecystectomy.

Lungs and pleura: Pronounced gravitational changes on both sides. Calcified granuloma in the right lower lobe, measures up to 2 mm (series 5, image 78) and requires no follow up. Linear atelectasis in the right upper lobe (series 5, images 100-115) against the background of secretion in a small bronchus (series 5, images 98-102). Linear atelectasis in the middle upper lobe (series 5, images 140-158), clinically insignificant. The walls of the subsegmental bronchi are minimally thickened. No consolidation or pleural effusion. 

Mediastinum and lung hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy. Calcification in a nonenlarged subcarinal lymph node.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Coronary calcification, qualitatively assessed as mild. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated. Aortic walls with a few calcified plaques. Pulmonary trunk is not dilated. 

Osseous structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. 

Included abdomen: Cholecystectomy.
val_impression:

2. Calcified granuloma in the right lower lobe and calcification of mediastinal lymph nodes, consistent with prior infection, require no follow up.

3. Coronary calcification, qualitatively assessed as mild. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

4. Mild atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume 11.84 mGy; total DLP 399.98 mGy-cm

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 0.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Pulmonary nodules in series 4 are as follows:

Right lower lobe:
- Part-solid subpleural nodule measuring 6 mm on image 99 (decreased in size, previously up to 1.4 × 0.8 cm).

Left upper lobe:
- Solid nodule measuring 4 mm along the interlobar fissure on image 58 (no change).
- Solid subpleural nodule measuring 5 mm on image 102 (no change).

In both lungs, solid micronodules measure up to 3 mm (no change) and calcified granulomas measure up to 6 mm. Moderate centrilobular and paraseptal emphysema in both lungs. Plate-like atelectasis in both lower lobes. No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and lung hila: No mass. No lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Coronary calcification, qualitatively assessed as severe. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

Great vessels: Dilation of the ascending thoracic aorta measuring up to 4.0 cm, severe wall calcification. Main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Osseous structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Diffuse heterogeneous decreased density of vertebral bodies (maximum down to 102 HU). Degenerative changes of the shoulder joints, more pronounced on the left.

Included upper abdomen: Unremarkable.
doc_impression:
1. Part-solid nodule in the right lower lobe. Per Fleischner Society 2017 guidelines, CT in 3–6 months is needed to confirm persistence. If unchanged and the solid component remains less than 6 mm, annual CT for 5 years is recommended. Solid nodules in the left upper lobe and micronodules in both lungs: per Fleischner Society 2017 guidelines, no follow-up is required.

2. Moderate centrilobular and paraseptal emphysema in both lungs.

3. Calcified granulomas in both lungs, consistent with prior infection.

4. Coronary calcification, qualitatively assessed as severe.

5. Dilation of the ascending thoracic aorta.

6. Decreased vertebral body density consistent with osteopenia/osteoporosis.

CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume {dose_ctdi} mGy; total DLP {dose_dlp} mGy-cm

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months:
val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Pulmonary nodules in series 4 are as follows:
Right lower lobe:
- Part-solid subpleural nodule measures 6 mm on image 99 (decreased in size, previously up to 1.4 × 0.8 cm).
Left upper lobe:
- Solid nodules measure 4 mm along the interlobar fissure on images 58 and 60 (no change).
- Solid subpleural nodule measures 5 mm on image 102 (no change).
In both lungs, centrilobular micronodules and ground-glass nodules measure up to 3 mm (no change). Calcified granulomas measure up to 6 mm in both lungs. Moderate centrilobular and paraseptal emphysema. Linear atelectasis in both lower lobes. No pleural effusion. Significant thickening of the walls of the subsegmental bronchi, including the upper lobes, which corresponds to bronchitis.

Mediastinum and lung hila: No mass. No lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Coronary calcification, qualitatively assessed as severe. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

Great vessels: Dilation of the ascending thoracic aorta measures up to 4.0 cm, with severe wall calcification. Main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Osseous structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Diffuse heterogeneous decreased density of vertebral bodies (maximum down to 102 HU). Degenerative changes of the shoulder joints, more pronounced on the left.

Included upper abdomen: Unremarkable.
val_impression:
1. Decreased in size, part-solid nodule, measuring 6 mm in the right lower lobe, may have an inflammatory etiology. According to Fleischner Society Recommendation, part-solid nodules may be due to transient infections and may resolve after short-term follow-up. Unchanged solid nodules in the left upper lobe require no follow up. Multiple centrilobular micronodules and ground-glass nodules may represent smoking-related disease (respiratory bronchiolitis). Significant thickening of the walls of the subsegmental bronchi, including the upper lobes, which corresponds to bronchitis.

2. Moderate centrilobular and paraseptal emphysema in both lungs. Pulmonary emphysema on CT is an independent risk factor for lung cancer. Consider enrollment in a low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening program if the patient meets eligibility criteria; today's CT may serve as a baseline for future screening.

3. Calcified granulomas in both lungs, consistent with prior infection. No follow-up is required.

4. Coronary calcification, qualitatively assessed as severe. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

5. Dilation of the ascending thoracic aorta, up to 4.0 cm.

6. Decreased vertebral body density consistent with osteopenia. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Degenerative changes of the shoulder joints.
